request.question: What award did the creator of the HTO receive in the WW?

title: Hermann Göring
Katsuhiro Otomo
Howard E. Greer
President's Volunteer Service Award
Charles Upham
Amory Adventure Award
Golden Globe Cecil B. DeMille Award
Commissioner's Historic Achievement Award
San Francisco World Spirits Competition
Haupttreuhandstelle Ost
sentences: Hermann Wilhelm Göring (or Goering; ] ; 12 January 1893 – 15 October 1946) was a German political and military leader as well as one of the most powerful figures in the Nazi Party (NSDAP) that ruled Germany from 1933 to 1945.
A veteran World War I fighter pilot ace, he was a recipient of the "Pour le Mérite".
He was the last commander of "Jagdgeschwader" 1, the fighter wing once led by Manfred von Richthofen.
Katsuhiro Otomo (大友 克洋 , Ōtomo Katsuhiro , born April 14, 1954) is a Japanese manga artist, screenwriter and film director.
He is best known as the creator of the manga "Akira" and its animated film adaptation.

He was decorated a "Chevalier" of the French Ordre des Arts et des Lettres in 2005, promoted to "Officier" of the order in 2014, became the fourth manga artist ever inducted into the American Eisner Award Hall of Fame in 2012, and was awarded the Purple Medal of Honor from the Japanese government in 2013.
Otomo later received the Winsor McCay Award at the 41st Annie Awards in 2014 and the 2015 Grand Prix de la ville d'Angoulême, the first manga artist to receive the award.
Howard Earl Greer (May 1, 1921 – November 22, 2015) was a vice admiral in the United States Navy.
He is a former commander of the USS "Hancock" (CV-19), chief of staff of Naval Air Force Pacific, commander Naval Air Reserve Force, and Commander, Naval Air Force U.S. Atlantic Fleet.
His awards include 2 Distinguished Service Medals, 4 Legions of Merit, the Navy Commendation Medal, Pacific Theater WW II Ribbon with 9 Battle Stars and the Vietnamese Distinguished Service Award.
He died in 2015.
The President's Volunteer Service Award is a civil award bestowed by the President of the United States.

Established by executive order by George W. Bush, the award was established to honor volunteers that give hundreds of hours per year helping others through the President's Council on Service and Civic Participation.
The award can be granted to individuals, families and organizations located throughout the United States.

Depending on the amount of service hours completed, individuals can receive the Bronze, Silver, Gold, and/or the President's Call to Service Award (also referred to the President's Lifetime Achievement Award).

The Call to Service Award is the most prestigious, and it has been awarded to few Americans to recognize over 4,000 hours of extraordinary service including honorees such as S. Truett Cathy, Mark Carman, Zach Bonner, Brandon Pugh, Thomas Smith, and Michael Taggart.
Awardees may receive a personalized certificate, an official pin, medallion, and/or a congratulatory letter from the President depending on the award earned.

Charles Hazlitt Upham, (21 September 1908 – 22 November 1994) was a New Zealand soldier who earned the Victoria Cross (VC) twice during the Second World War; in Crete in May 1941, and at Ruweisat Ridge, Egypt, in July 1942.
He was the most recent of only three people to receive the VC twice, the only one to receive two VCs during the Second World War and the only combat soldier to receive the award twice.
As a result, Upham is often described as the most highly decorated Commonwealth soldier of that war, as the VC is the Commonwealth's highest award for gallantry in the face of the enemy.
The Amory Adventure Award is an award in the Canadian Venturer program.
Unlike all other awards and badges, for example the Queen's Venturer Award, the Amory Award is only granted to one Venturer company each year.
All Venturers who take part in an Amory Award expedition (whether or not they are members of the winning company) receive a participation badge to wear on the uniform.
The first-place company's name is recorded on the Amory Adventure Trophy, which the company may keep for nine months.
Companies that place first, second, and third each receive a trophy of their own to keep.
The Cecil B. DeMille Award is an honorary Golden Globe Award bestowed by the Hollywood Foreign Press Association for "outstanding contributions to the world of entertainment".
It was first presented on February 21, 1952 at the 9th Annual Golden Globe Awards ceremony and is named in honor of its first recipient, director Cecil B. DeMille.
Honorees are selected by the HFPA board of directors and are presented annually (except for 1976 and 2008).

The first woman to receive the honor was Judy Garland in 1962 who, at 39 years of age, was also the youngest honoree ever to receive the award, while Samuel Goldwyn, at the age of 93, in 1973, was the oldest.
The first African-American to receive the honor was Sidney Poitier in 1982.
The Commissioner's Historic Achievement Award is awarded by the Commissioner of Baseball to a group or person who has made a "major impact on the sport" of baseball.
It is not an annual award; rather, the Commissioner presents the trophy at his discretion.

The trophy is a gold baseball sitting atop a cylindrical silver base, created by Tiffany & Co. The award has been presented fourteen times by Commissioner Bud Selig: eleven times to players, once to a team, and twice to a non-player.
Mark McGwire and Sammy Sosa were the first to receive the award for their parts in the 1998 MLB home run record chase.
The most recent recipient is Derek Jeter (2014), who was honored in the final year of his career for being "one of the most accomplished shortstops of all-time".
The 2001 Seattle Mariners won the award as a team for posting a 116–46 record one season after losing Alex Rodriguez to the Texas Rangers.
Roberto Clemente, the 2006 awardee, is the only player to receive the award posthumously; his award was accepted by his wife, Vera.
The San Francisco World Spirits Competition was founded in 2000 by Anthony Dias Blue.
Blue is the current director of both the San Francisco World Spirits Competition and the San Francisco International Wine Competition.

It assesses hundreds of entrants annually (1,407 in 2013 from 63 countries) with tastings involving panels of expert judges selected each year from the spirits industry including world-renowned mixologists, spirits buyers, and media from across the United States.
Producers must submit their product for the competition and pay a fee ($475 for 2013) for its evaluation.
Not all entries are given awards (those not judged of sufficient quality are not given an award) but most receive a bronze, silver, or gold award from the tasting panel.

The fact that most entrants receive an award likely involves some degree of self-selection, as the spirits producers choose whether to enter each of their brands in the competition and pay to receive a rating.
Those entrants that are given a unanimous gold medal by the panel are given the distinction of a "double-gold" medal.
Additionally, a "best in show" designation is awarded in each main category of spirits.

The Haupttreuhandstelle Ost (HTO), or the Main Trustee Office for the East, created by Hermann Göring, was a Nazi German predatory state institution responsible for liquidating Polish and Jewish businesses in occupied Poland or selling them to German "settlers" from the East for a symbolic fee during World War II.
It was staffed by "a veritable stampede" of officials from the Deutsche and Dresdner banks.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- If summary_1 already includes explicit profession/role/descriptor tokens for an anchor (for example, 'singer', 'director', 'country music artist'), omit those redundant descriptor tokens and emit a compact anchor-only query consisting of the core visible anchors (names, titles, groups). Retain relation tokens only when the question explicitly requests them or when disambiguation rules demand them. Do not remove descriptors when the question explicitly asks for role/occupation evidence or when a short-title disambiguation rule requires adding an entertainment disambiguator.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Disambiguation for short/ambiguous title tokens:
- If an anchor in summary_1 is a short or potentially ambiguous title token, do not automatically append an entertainment-domain keyword. Only append a plain entertainment disambiguator keyword (for example: film movie album song novel author director) when the original question itself contains explicit entertainment-role words (e.g., film, movie, album, song, directed, author, novella) or when the question explicitly lists multiple title-like tokens. In those cases, include the visible anchor(s) from summary_1 plus the appropriate entertainment keyword to bias retrieval. If the question lacks entertainment cues and does not list multiple titles, do not guess or add an entertainment disambiguator—keep the query anchored to the visible tokens only.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- When the context contains a concise bridge claim about a named entity (or otherwise names candidate/bridge entities), treat the context claim as the summary anchor and perform explicit identity-alignment checks before using passage facts. For each entity explicitly named in `context` as a candidate or bridge, do the following: if at least one passage provides an explicit, identity-aligned statement linking that named entity (or a clearly indicated alias) to the asserted relation, append a single brief corroborating sentence that paraphrases that linking fact and references the corroborating passage and also include one short clause for that context-named entity giving the passage-supported relation value (e.g., "Name: [relation value]."); if no passage provides the relation value for that context-named entity, append a short clause stating that no relation value is provided in the evidence (e.g., "Name: no [relation] provided in evidence."). Do not create such clauses for entities that are only mentioned in `passages` and are not named in `context`. Do not append corroboration if the `context` already contains the identical corroborating fact verbatim. Keep these additions short and keep the overall summary compact.
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.
- When the summaries contain both a short single-token alias and, in the later integrated summary (summary_2), a contiguous multi-token full-name literal that explicitly identifies or is explicitly linked to the same person (for example via an identity link or biographical framing in summary_2), prefer and emit that multi-token full-name literal exactly as it appears in summary_2. Do not prefer the longer form unless summary_2 provides an explicit identity link tying the alias to the full-name literal; if no such explicit link is present, follow the base identity-link rules and do not force selection of the longer form.
- Vocal-role single-token normalization (narrow scope): If summary_2 (alone or composed with summary_1 where identity links are explicit) explicitly contains vocal-role evidence — i.e., one or more verbalized vocal-role phrases that clearly denote a singing performer (examples: "lead singer", "lead vocals", "vocalist" as presented in the summaries) — then you may prefer to emit the concise single-token common noun for a person who sings (e.g., "singer") only when that single-token common noun itself appears verbatim in the summaries or when the vocal-role phrases in the summaries unambiguously denote the same person-role. If those explicit lexical licenses are not present, retain the literal role phrasing as given in summary_2 and do not normalize modifiers or drop qualifiers.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: What award did the creator of the HTO receive in the WW?
hop1_docs: I Saw What You Did (1988 film) | I Saw What You Did is a 1988 American made-for-television horror film directed by Fred Walton.  It is a remake of the 1965 theatrical film of the same name starring Joan Crawford.  It received generally negative reviews, with only a few exceptions.  Nevertheless, it won an Emmy Award in the category "Outstanding Cinematography for a Miniseries or a Special".

Tax wedge | The tax wedge is the deviation from the equilibrium price/quantity (formula_1 and formula_2, respectively) as a result of the taxation of a good.  Because of the tax, consumers pay more for the good (formula_3) than they did before the tax, and suppliers receive less for the good (formula_4) than they did before the tax .  Put differently, the tax wedge is the difference between what consumers pay and what producers receive (net of tax) from a transaction.  The tax effectively drives a "wedge" between the price consumers pay and the price producers receive for a product.
HTO Park | HTO Park (stylized as HO) is an urban beach in Toronto, Ontario, Canada, that opened in 2007.  It is located west of Harbourfront Centre, on Lake Ontario.

Kenny Hotz | Kenneth Joel "Kenny" Hotz is a Canadian comedy writer, producer, director, actor and comedian.  Hotz is a "South Park" consultant and writer, creator/star of the Comedy Central television show "Kenny vs. Spenny", creator and writer of the FX series "Testees", and the creator/star of "Kenny Hotz's Triumph of the Will".  He has received numerous awards for his television work— from the Academy of Canadian Cinema & Television the Gemini Awards and the Canadian Comedy Awards—and is a multiple-time film festival award-winner.  He is also an award-winning "Vice" contributor and Gulf war photo-journalist.

Panel Syndicate | Panel Syndicate is an online publisher of DRM-free pay what you want webcomics in multiple languages, founded by Marcos Martín to publish his and Brian K. Vaughan's creator-owned comic "The Private Eye" in March 2013.  To date Panel Syndicate has published comics in English, Spanish, Catalan, Portuguese, and French, with additional languages in development.  "The Private Eye" has received critical acclaim and media attention for its role as one of the first DRM-free, pay what you want comics by creators of Martín and Vaughan's caliber.  Initially an outlet for publishing Vaughan and Martín's collaborations, Panel Syndicate is open to publishing other creators' works by using the same DRM-free, pay what you want model.

What Richard Did | What Richard Did is a 2012 Irish film directed by Lenny Abrahamson and written by Malcolm Campbell.  The film is loosely based on Kevin Power's "Bad Day in Blackrock", a fictionalised novel inspired by the real-life death of Brian Murphy in 2000.  It won the best Irish film of the year award at the 10th Irish Film & Television Awards and was the most commercially successful Irish film of 2012.

Lena Dunham | Lena Dunham ( ; born May 13, 1986) is an American actress, writer, producer, and director.  She is best known as the creator, writer and star of the HBO series "Girls" (2012–2017), for which she has received numerous Emmy Award nominations and two Golden Globe Awards. Dunham's work on "Girls" also led her to become the first woman to win the Directors Guild of America Award for Outstanding Directing – Comedy Series in 2013.  That year Dunham was included in the "Time" 100 list of the most influential people in the world.  In 2014, Dunham released her first book, "Not That Kind of Girl: A Young Woman Tells You What She's "Learned"".
hop1_titles: I Saw What You Did (1988 film)
response.summary_1:
- Evidence: "HTO Park (stylized as HO) is an urban beach in Toronto, Ontario, Canada" (passage [3]).  
- Evidence: Kenneth Joel "Kenny" Hotz is "creator/star of 'Kenny Hotz's Triumph of the Will' ... has received numerous awards ... the Gemini Awards and the Canadian Comedy Awards" (passage [4]).  
- Missing / bridge: passages do not state who "the creator of the HTO" (i.e., whether HTO refers to HTO Park or to "Kenny Hotz's Triumph of the Will") is, nor do they state what "WW" denotes. No passage links Kenny Hotz to HTO Park.  
- Next-hop retrieval direction: (1) determine which HTO the question means (search "creator of HTO Park" and "creator of 'Kenny Hotz's Triumph of the Will'"); (2) once the creator is identified, search "[creator name] award WW" or include likely expansions of "WW" (e.g., "World War," "What Women," "WW" as a publication/organization) to find which award they "received in the WW."
response.hop2_query: HTO Park creator OR Kenny Hotz Triumph of the Will creator
hop2_docs: Kenny Hotz | Kenneth Joel "Kenny" Hotz is a Canadian comedy writer, producer, director, actor and comedian.  Hotz is a "South Park" consultant and writer, creator/star of the Comedy Central television show "Kenny vs. Spenny", creator and writer of the FX series "Testees", and the creator/star of "Kenny Hotz's Triumph of the Will".  He has received numerous awards for his television work— from the Academy of Canadian Cinema & Television the Gemini Awards and the Canadian Comedy Awards—and is a multiple-time film festival award-winner.  He is also an award-winning "Vice" contributor and Gulf war photo-journalist.
Kenny Hotz's Triumph of the Will | Kenny Hotz's Triumph of the Will is a Canadian television series, which debuted on July 22, 2011, on Action.

Kenny vs. Spenny vs. Canada | Kenny vs. Spenny vs. Canada is a comedy tour starring Kenny Hotz and Spencer Rice, creators of the Canadian television series "Kenny vs. Spenny".  The tour began on March 31, 2014, in Vancouver, British Columbia, and concluded on May 15 in Thunder Bay, Ontario.  At these 14 shows, Hotz and Rice presented rare and unseen footage from "Kenny vs. Spenny" reels which broadcasters considered too graphic or outrageous to air on television.  They also discussed their origins in the entertainment business and answered questions from the audience.  Bobby Patton, an occasional guest on "Kenny vs. Spenny", and other members of the show's crew accompanied Hotz and Rice on the tour.

List of Kenny vs. Spenny episodes | "Kenny vs. Spenny" is a Canadian reality comedy series which follows the lives of friends Kenny Hotz and Spencer Rice who face each other in various competitions.  The loser of each competition must perform an act of humiliation.  The humiliation is selected by the winner of each competition unless the competition ends in draw, whereby the film crew decides what act of humiliation both Hotz and Rice must perform.  The series is filmed in the house that both Hotz and Rice share in Toronto, Ontario.
HTO Park | HTO Park (stylized as HO) is an urban beach in Toronto, Ontario, Canada, that opened in 2007.  It is located west of Harbourfront Centre, on Lake Ontario.

South Park (season 3) | The third season of "South Park", an American animated television comedy series, originally aired in the United States on Comedy Central between April 7, 1999 and January 12, 2000.  The season was headed by the series creators Trey Parker and Matt Stone, who served as executive producers along with Anne Garefino.  The season continued to focus on the exploits of protagonists Stan, Kyle, Cartman, and Kenny in the fictional Colorado mountain town of South Park.

Kenny McCormick | Kenneth "Kenny" McCormick is a main character in the animated adult television series "South Park", along with his friends Stan Marsh, Kyle Broflovski, and Eric Cartman.  His oft-muffled and indiscernible speech—the result of his parka hood covering his mouth—is provided by co-creator Matt Stone.  He debuted on television when "South Park" first aired on August 13, 1997, after having first appeared in "The Spirit of Christmas" shorts created by Stone and long-time collaborator Trey Parker in 1992 ("Jesus vs. Frosty") and 1995 ("Jesus vs. Santa").
